messages.content: Build a generic Effect utility `traceEffect<A, E, R>(label: string, eff: Effect.Effect<A, E, R>)` that logs on both success and failure while preserving A, E, R.
How do I write a reusable `withDefault<A, E, R>` that replaces failures of an Effect with a default value while preserving the environment channel?
Explain briefly why a generic Effect utility must keep its type parameters polymorphic, then implement a `firstSuccess<A, E, R>` that takes a NonEmptyArray of effects and yields the first to succeed.
Write a generic helper `mapBoth` that transforms both the success and error channels of any `Effect.Effect<A, E, R>` using two pure functions.
Implement a generic `timed<A, E, R>` Effect utility that returns `[durationMs, A]` after running the inner effect, preserving E and R.
Build a generic `retryWithLog<A, E, R>(eff, times)` that retries an Effect N times, logging each attempt, returning `Effect<A, E, R>` with the same parameters.

```typescript
import { Effect, Schedule, Console, pipe } from "effect"

export const retryWithLog = <A, E, R>(
  eff: Effect.Effect<A, E, R>,
  times: number
): Effect.Effect<A, E, R> =>
  pipe(
    eff,
    Effect.tapError((e) => Console.error("attempt failed:", e)),
    Effect.retry(Schedule.recurs(times))
  )

const flaky: Effect.Effect<number, string, never> = Effect.fail("nope")
void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(retryWithLog(flaky, 2)))
```
Show the v2 fp-ts HKT surface by implementing a function `mapHKT` that works for any URI in URItoKind, using the Functor1 type class. Keep it type-safe with no any.

```typescript
import { URIS, Kind } from "fp-ts/HKT"
import { Functor1 } from "fp-ts/Functor"
import * as O from "fp-ts/Option"
import * as A from "fp-ts/Array"

export const mapHKT =
  <F extends URIS>(F: Functor1<F>) =>
  <A, B>(fa: Kind<F, A>, f: (a: A) => B): Kind<F, B> =>
    F.map(fa, f)

const someN: O.Option<number> = O.some(2)
const mappedO = mapHKT(O.Functor)(someN, (n) => n + 1)
const mappedA = mapHKT(A.Functor)([1, 2, 3], (n) => n * 2)
void mappedO
void mappedA
```
Explain briefly how fp-ts URItoKind lets you abstract over Option and Either, then show a `liftPredicate` helper parametrised over a Functor1 URI.

fp-ts encodes higher-kinded types through a phantom `URItoKind` map: each functor registers a URI string to its concrete type constructor. `Kind<F, A>` then looks the URI up and substitutes `A`, giving you one abstraction that works for `Option<A>`, `Either<E, A>` (via `Kind2`), `Array<A>`, etc. A `Functor1<F>` says "F is a unary type constructor with `map`"; armed with it you can write combinators once and run them against every registered instance.

```typescript
import { URIS, Kind } from "fp-ts/HKT"
import { Functor1 } from "fp-ts/Functor"
import * as O from "fp-ts/Option"

export const mapped =
  <F extends URIS>(F: Functor1<F>) =>
  <A>(fa: Kind<F, A>, p: (a: A) => boolean): Kind<F, boolean> =>
    F.map(fa, p)

const r = mapped(O.Functor)(O.some(3), (n) => n > 0)
void r
```
Write a generic fp-ts helper `sequence1<F, A>` that takes a `Functor1<F>` and turns `Kind<F, A>` into `Kind<F, readonly [A]>`, demonstrating HKT at work.

```typescript
import { URIS, Kind } from "fp-ts/HKT"
import { Functor1 } from "fp-ts/Functor"
import * as O from "fp-ts/Option"
import * as A from "fp-ts/Array"

export const sequence1 =
  <F extends URIS>(F: Functor1<F>) =>
  <A>(fa: Kind<F, A>): Kind<F, readonly [A]> =>
    F.map(fa, (a) => [a] as const)

const o = sequence1(O.Functor)(O.some(1))
const arr = sequence1(A.Functor)([1, 2])
void o
void arr
```
Fix this fp-ts HKT snippet — it uses `any` instead of proper Kind inference: `const lift = <F>(F: any) => <A, B>(f: (a: A) => B) => (fa: any) => F.map(fa, f)`.

```typescript
import { URIS, Kind } from "fp-ts/HKT"
import { Functor1 } from "fp-ts/Functor"
import * as E from "fp-ts/Either"

export const lift =
  <F extends URIS>(F: Functor1<F>) =>
  <A, B>(f: (a: A) => B) =>
  (fa: Kind<F, A>): Kind<F, B> =>
    F.map(fa, f)

const rightV: E.Either<string, number> = E.right(10)
void rightV
```
Using fp-ts HKT, build an abstract `countTrue<F>` for any Foldable1 instance that counts elements matching a predicate.

```typescript
import { URIS, Kind } from "fp-ts/HKT"
import { Foldable1 } from "fp-ts/Foldable"
import * as A from "fp-ts/Array"
import * as O from "fp-ts/Option"
import { pipe } from "fp-ts/function"

export const countTrue =
  <F extends URIS>(F: Foldable1<F>) =>
  <A>(fa: Kind<F, A>, p: (a: A) => boolean): number =>
    F.reduce(fa, 0, (acc, a) => (p(a) ? acc + 1 : acc))

const c = countTrue(A.Foldable)([1, 2, 3, 4], (n) => n % 2 === 0)
const guarded = pipe(O.some(c), O.chain((n) => (n > 0 ? O.some(n) : O.none)))
void guarded
```
Build a typed ADT `Shape` using `Data.TaggedEnum` with Circle, Square, Rectangle constructors, plus an `area` matcher.
How do I declare a typed record with `Data.struct` so structural equality works out of the box?
Explain briefly how `Data.case` differs from a hand-written constructor, then implement a `User` case class with `id` and `name`.
Write an Event ADT with `Data.taggedEnum` containing `Click`, `Hover`, `Submit`, then an exhaustive handler returning a log string.
Build a `Money` ADT using `Data.struct` that stores cents plus ISO currency, and a typed `add` that only accepts same-currency values.

```typescript
import { Data, Effect } from "effect"

interface Money {
  readonly cents: number
  readonly currency: "USD" | "EUR"
}
export const Money = (cents: number, currency: Money["currency"]): Money =>
  Data.struct({ cents, currency })

export const add = (
  a: Money,
  b: Money
): Effect.Effect<Money, "CurrencyMismatch"> =>
  a.currency === b.currency
    ? Effect.succeed(Money(a.cents + b.cents, a.currency))
    : Effect.fail("CurrencyMismatch")

void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(add(Money(100, "USD"), Money(50, "USD"))))
```
Add a `toJSON` method-style helper for this `Data.case` class (in snippet form), keeping strict types: `interface Order { id: string; total: number } const Order = Data.case<Order>()`.
Show the variance of `Effect.Effect<A, E, R>` — build two services and demonstrate that R is contravariant (a narrower requirement flows up).

Explain briefly why the E channel in `Effect<A, E, R>` is covariant, then demonstrate with a value of type `Effect<number, "A" | "B", never>` accepted where `Effect<number, string, never>` is expected.

E is produced by the effect, not consumed, so it varies like a return type: a narrower E (say `"A" | "B"`) is assignable to a wider E (`string`) because every error you might raise is still a string. This is the same reasoning that makes `() => "A"` assignable to `() => string`. Upcasting the error channel is safe; downcasting is not.

```typescript
import { Effect } from "effect"

const narrow: Effect.Effect<number, "A" | "B", never> = Effect.fail("A")
const wide: Effect.Effect<number, string, never> = narrow
void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(wide))
```
Write a small program that illustrates the contravariance of R: combine `Effect<A, never, Logger>` with `Effect<A, never, Logger | DB>` and show the combined R is the union.

Build a variance-demo: two tagged error classes FooError and BarError, an Effect that can raise either, and show an `Effect<never, FooError, never>` assigning into `Effect<never, FooError | BarError, never>`.
What's the Effect way to keep the R channel `never` after providing every required service? Show a minimal example.
Implement an Effect-TS refinement `isNonEmpty` that narrows `ReadonlyArray<string>` to a `ReadonlyNonEmptyArray<string>` type alias.

```typescript
import { Effect } from "effect"

type NonEmpty<A> = readonly [A, ...A[]]

export const isNonEmpty = <A>(xs: ReadonlyArray<A>): xs is NonEmpty<A> =>
  xs.length > 0

export const head = <A>(xs: ReadonlyArray<A>): Effect.Effect<A, "Empty"> =>
  isNonEmpty(xs) ? Effect.succeed(xs[0]) : Effect.fail("Empty")

void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(head(["a", "b"])))
```
How do I narrow an `unknown` input to `{ kind: "ok"; value: number }` using a refinement inside an Effect pipeline?

```typescript
import { Effect } from "effect"

type Ok = { readonly kind: "ok"; readonly value: number }

const isOk = (u: unknown): u is Ok =>
  typeof u === "object" && u !== null &&
  (u as { kind?: unknown }).kind === "ok" &&
  typeof (u as { value?: unknown }).value === "number"

export const parseOk = (u: unknown): Effect.Effect<Ok, "BadInput"> =>
  isOk(u) ? Effect.succeed(u) : Effect.fail("BadInput")

void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(parseOk({ kind: "ok", value: 7 })))
```
Explain briefly what a predicate refinement buys you over a boolean check, then show an `Effect.filterOrFail` that uses a refinement to narrow the success channel.

A refinement (`(x: A) => x is B`) teaches the compiler a new fact: if the predicate returns true, the value really is B. A plain boolean predicate is opaque — it can guard a branch but the type stays A. With a refinement, `Effect.filterOrFail` (or a plain if) can downcast the success channel from `A` to `B` statically, so subsequent steps can rely on the narrower shape without further casts.

```typescript
import { Effect, pipe } from "effect"

type Positive = number & { readonly _brand: "Positive" }
const isPositive = (n: number): n is Positive => n > 0

export const onlyPositive = (n: number): Effect.Effect<Positive, "NotPositive"> =>
  pipe(Effect.succeed(n), Effect.filterOrFail(isPositive, () => "NotPositive" as const))

void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(onlyPositive(5)))
```
Write a fp-ts Either refinement helper `fromPredicateRefined` that preserves the narrowed type in the Right channel.
Build a chained refinement pipeline using pipe that narrows `unknown` -> `string` -> `NonEmptyString`, and short-circuits with a tagged error at the first failure.

```typescript
import { Effect, pipe } from "effect"

type NonEmpty = string & { readonly _brand: "NonEmpty" }
const isString = (u: unknown): u is string => typeof u === "string"
const isNonEmpty = (s: string): s is NonEmpty => s.length > 0

export const parse = (u: unknown): Effect.Effect<NonEmpty, "NotString" | "Empty"> =>
  pipe(
    Effect.succeed(u),
    Effect.filterOrFail(isString, () => "NotString" as const),
    Effect.flatMap((s) =>
      isNonEmpty(s) ? Effect.succeed(s) : Effect.fail("Empty" as const)
    )
  )

void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(parse("ok")))
```
Refactor this to use a refinement instead of a cast: `const pickStrings = (xs: unknown[]) => xs.filter(x => typeof x === 'string') as string[]`.

```typescript
import * as O from "fp-ts/Option"
import { pipe } from "fp-ts/function"

const isString = (u: unknown): u is string => typeof u === "string"

export const pickStrings = (xs: ReadonlyArray<unknown>): ReadonlyArray<string> =>
  xs.filter(isString)

const sample: ReadonlyArray<string> = pickStrings([1, "a", true, "b"])
export const firstStr = pipe(
  sample.length > 0 ? O.some(sample[0]) : O.none,
  O.map((s) => s.toUpperCase())
)
void firstStr
```
Define a conditional type `ExtractError<E>` that unwraps `Effect.Effect<A, E, R>` to its E channel, and demonstrate it.

```typescript
import { Effect } from "effect"

export type ExtractError<T> = T extends Effect.Effect<infer _A, infer E, infer _R> ? E : never

const prog: Effect.Effect<number, "BadInput" | "Timeout", never> = Effect.fail("Timeout")
type Err = ExtractError<typeof prog>

const handled: Effect.Effect<number, never, never> = Effect.catchAll(prog, (_e: Err) => Effect.succeed(0))
void Effect.runPromise(handled)
```
How do I write a conditional type `ExtractSuccess<T>` and `ExtractRequirement<T>` for Effect values? Show them being used.

```typescript
import { Effect, Context, Layer } from "effect"

export type ExtractSuccess<T> = T extends Effect.Effect<infer A, infer _E, infer _R> ? A : never
export type ExtractRequirement<T> = T extends Effect.Effect<infer _A, infer _E, infer R> ? R : never

interface Cfg { readonly env: string }
const Cfg = Context.GenericTag<Cfg>("app/Cfg")

const prog = Effect.gen(function* () {
  const c = yield* Cfg
  return c.env
})

type A = ExtractSuccess<typeof prog>
type R = ExtractRequirement<typeof prog>
const _a: A = "dev"
const _r: R = { env: "dev" } as unknown as R
void Effect.runPromise(Effect.provide(prog, Layer.succeed(Cfg, { env: "dev" })))
```
Explain briefly how a distributive conditional type reacts to a union, then implement `EffectsOfUnion<U>` that distributes over a union of Effects.

Conditional types in TypeScript are distributive when the checked type is a naked type parameter: `T extends X ? ... : ...` applies element-wise to each member of a union `T`. This lets you transform `A | B | C` into a union of mapped results with no extra machinery. Wrap the parameter in `[T]` to disable distribution when you want to treat the union as a single type.

```typescript
import { Effect } from "effect"

export type EffectsOfUnion<U> = U extends infer T ? Effect.Effect<T, never, never> : never

type Prims = "a" | 1 | true
type Es = EffectsOfUnion<Prims>

const e1: Es = Effect.succeed("a")
const e2: Es = Effect.succeed(1)
void Effect.runPromise(e1)
void Effect.runPromise(e2)
```
Build a `UnionOfErrors<Tuple>` conditional that extracts the union of error channels from a tuple of Effects.

```typescript
import { Effect } from "effect"

type ErrorOf<T> = T extends Effect.Effect<infer _A, infer E, infer _R> ? E : never
export type UnionOfErrors<Tup extends ReadonlyArray<Effect.Effect<unknown, unknown, unknown>>> =
  { [K in keyof Tup]: ErrorOf<Tup[K]> }[number]

const a: Effect.Effect<number, "X", never> = Effect.fail("X")
const b: Effect.Effect<number, "Y", never> = Effect.fail("Y")
type Errs = UnionOfErrors<[typeof a, typeof b]>
const _e: Errs = "X"
void _e
```
Constraint-driven: write a conditional utility `IsFailable<T>` that yields `true` when an Effect has a non-never E channel, `false` otherwise. Strict types, no any.

```typescript
import { Effect } from "effect"

export type IsFailable<T> = T extends Effect.Effect<infer _A, infer E, infer _R>
  ? [E] extends [never] ? false : true
  : never

const pure: Effect.Effect<number, never, never> = Effect.succeed(1)
const fail: Effect.Effect<number, "E", never> = Effect.fail("E")

const _p: IsFailable<typeof pure> = false
const _f: IsFailable<typeof fail> = true
void _p
void _f
```
Add a `WithoutRequirements<T>` helper that rewrites any `Effect<A, E, R>` to `Effect<A, E, never>` at the type level. Show it narrowing a concrete effect type.
Build a branded `Email` type with Effect's Brand module and a Schema-backed constructor that validates format.

```typescript
import { Brand, Effect, pipe } from "effect"

export type Email = string & Brand.Brand<"Email">
const EmailBrand = Brand.refined<Email>(
  (s) => /.+@.+\..+/.test(s),
  (s) => Brand.error(`not an email: ${s}`)
)

export const parseEmail = (s: string): Effect.Effect<Email, "InvalidEmail"> =>
  pipe(
    Effect.try({ try: () => EmailBrand(s), catch: () => "InvalidEmail" as const })
  )

const program = parseEmail("ada@example.com")
void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(program))
```
How do I define nominal `UserId` and `OrderId` brands so the compiler rejects passing one where the other is expected?
Explain briefly why phantom-typed brands prevent primitive confusion, then show a `PositiveInt` brand built with `Brand.refined`.

A branded type is just the underlying primitive plus a phantom marker the structural type system treats as distinct. At runtime the value is still a plain number / string, but the compiler refuses to mix `PositiveInt` with a bare `number` because the brand tags differ. `Brand.refined` attaches a runtime check at the constructor, so the only way to get a `PositiveInt` is through that gate — a trust boundary expressed purely in types.

```typescript
import { Brand, Effect } from "effect"

export type PositiveInt = number & Brand.Brand<"PositiveInt">
export const PositiveInt = Brand.refined<PositiveInt>(
  (n) => Number.isInteger(n) && n > 0,
  (n) => Brand.error(`not positive int: ${n}`)
)

const increment = (n: PositiveInt): Effect.Effect<PositiveInt> =>
  Effect.succeed(PositiveInt(n + 1))
const v = PositiveInt(3)
void Effect.runPromise(increment(v))
```
Add a `NonEmptyString` brand to this existing function so the empty-string check is moved into the type system: `const greet = (name: string) => `hi ${name}``.

```typescript
import { Brand, Effect } from "effect"

export type NonEmptyString = string & Brand.Brand<"NonEmptyString">
export const NonEmptyString = Brand.refined<NonEmptyString>(
  (s) => s.length > 0,
  (s) => Brand.error(`empty: ${JSON.stringify(s)}`)
)

export const greet = (name: NonEmptyString): Effect.Effect<string> =>
  Effect.succeed(`hi ${name}`)

const n = NonEmptyString("ada")
void Effect.runPromise(greet(n))
```
Write an Effect v3 pipeline that parses `unknown` into a branded `Url` using a refined brand and a `pipe` chain.

```typescript
import { Brand, Effect, pipe } from "effect"

export type Url = string & Brand.Brand<"Url">
const UrlBrand = Brand.refined<Url>(
  (s) => { try { new URL(s); return true } catch { return false } },
  (s) => Brand.error(`bad url: ${s}`)
)

export const parseUrl = (u: unknown): Effect.Effect<Url, "NotString" | "BadUrl"> =>
  pipe(
    Effect.succeed(u),
    Effect.filterOrFail((x): x is string => typeof x === "string", () => "NotString" as const),
    Effect.flatMap((s) =>
      Effect.try({ try: () => UrlBrand(s), catch: () => "BadUrl" as const })
    )
  )

void Effect.runPromise(Effect.either(parseUrl("https://example.com")))
```
Targeted fix: this branded constructor uses `as` which defeats the point — rewrite with `Brand.nominal`: `type SKU = string & { _brand: 'SKU' }; const mk = (s: string) => s as SKU`.

```typescript
import { Brand, Effect, pipe } from "effect"

export type SKU = string & Brand.Brand<"SKU">
export const SKU = Brand.nominal<SKU>()

export const mkSku = (s: string): Effect.Effect<SKU> =>
  pipe(Effect.succeed(s), Effect.map((x) => SKU(x)))

void Effect.runPromise(mkSku("ABC-1"))
```
